title: Quiz: Quantum Advantage in Machine Learning
description: Test your understanding of quantum advantage concepts and limitations

questions.question: What is the data loading problem in QML?

options.text: Quantum computers cannot store data
Encoding classical data into quantum states may negate speedups
Quantum data is incompatible with classical storage
Loading data takes too much memory

questions.explanation: The data loading problem refers to the computational cost of encoding classical data into quantum states. If loading N values requires O(N) operations, exponential quantum speedups may be reduced to polynomial at best.

questions.question: What is a barren plateau in variational quantum algorithms?

options.text: A region where qubits lose coherence
A phenomenon where gradients vanish exponentially with qubit count
A type of quantum error
A limitation in classical optimizers

questions.explanation: Barren plateaus occur when the variance of gradients vanishes exponentially with the number of qubits, making gradient-based optimization infeasible for large systems.

questions.question: The HHL algorithm provides exponential speedup for all linear systems problems in machine learning.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. HHL has significant caveats: the matrix must be sparse and well-conditioned, input state preparation may be costly, and the full solution cannot be efficiently read out. These often eliminate the advantage.

questions.question: Which type of problem is most likely to show quantum advantage in ML?

options.text: Processing classical images from the internet
Learning from quantum experimental data
Natural language processing
Classical time series prediction

questions.explanation: Learning from quantum data avoids the classical data encoding bottleneck and leverages quantum systems' natural ability to process quantum states. This is where quantum advantage is most clearly established.

questions.question: What is 'dequantization' in the context of QML?

options.text: Converting quantum algorithms to run on classical computers
Removing quantum noise from circuits
Classical algorithms inspired by quantum that achieve similar speedup
Reducing the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: Dequantization refers to developing classical algorithms (often inspired by quantum techniques) that achieve comparable speedups, thereby eliminating the quantum advantage for certain problems.

questions.question: Which are valid criticisms of many QML advantage claims? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Comparisons against weak classical baselines
Testing on synthetic data designed to favor quantum
Not accounting for data loading costs
Using too many qubits

questions.explanation: Many QML papers compare against weak baselines, use artificial datasets, and ignore data loading costs. Using 'too many qubits' is not a common criticism - usually the opposite (too few) is the issue.

questions.question: Why might quantum kernels provide advantage for certain problems?

options.text: They compute faster on any data
They can access exponentially large feature spaces
They don't require training
They are immune to noise

questions.explanation: Quantum kernels compute inner products in Hilbert spaces of dimension 2^n for n qubits, potentially accessing feature spaces that are exponentially large compared to classical approaches.
